data.title: SCHB: The Conflict Between Short- And Long-Term Risks
data.description:
The Schwab U.S. Broad Market ETF (SCHB), launched on 11/03/2009 and managed by Charles Schwab Investment Management, Inc., provides exposure to the broad U.S. equity market. It has $39 billion in AUM and charges an expense ratio of 0.03%.

In the recent past, I have written a few ETF reviews about funds like SCHB, which seem likely to continue enjoying the current market euphoria in the short term. The last time I covered SCHB, my hold rating was based on the macroeconomic conditions that didn't warrant broad-market exposure when it came to equities. Since some of the near-term risks that I noted back then are not as big now, SCHB has become a bit more attractive and should keep outperforming alternative strategies such as value investing.

However, I have recently become more interested in the long-term implications, so I will devote some time to talking about the problems that being overweight equities introduces.

Portfolio

First, let me explain why I think that SCHB and other ETFs offering broad-market exposure will outperform alternative equity funds. First, notice the meaningful weights that Nvidia (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT) have in the portfolio. These operate businesses with non-cyclical drivers and this is a great benefit right now.

Tech stocks in general are less sensitive to cyclical demand and one-third of your equity portfolio consisting of them is a virtue instead of a risk these days.

The core idea is that economic growth is expected to be below historical averages for some time before it recovers. Recency bias can cause many market participants to believe that below-trend growth risk is almost non-existent, as Real GDP growth has increased for two consecutive quarters.

However, because of the contraction in the first quarter of 2025, average growth for the first half of that year was ~1.4%. The other thing to remember is that the growth in the second and third quarters can be explained by businesses cutting back on imports due to front-loading activity in the first quarter. It's too soon to deem the unusually high GDP growth not noisy. The economists' forecasts certainly remain consistent with that thesis, as you can see in the chart above.

This introduces a subtle but important risk for other market segments like mid-cap and small-cap stocks or equal-weighted portfolios that don't have a big tech bias. SCHB is now more attractive precisely because what constituted a risk in a more uncertain environment is now an advantage that can help it outperform alternative equity portfolios that are considered defensive.

Performance

But here's something for investors who are risk-averse to consider. A strong bias towards equities is still unwarranted. Some conditions have changed, making broad exposure more preferable than some strategies like value or dividend investing in the near term, as I already noted, but when it comes to relative attractiveness across assets, the outlook encourages caution.

In the last 12 months, SCHB did not significantly outperform 10-Year Treasuries like in other years because of high rates and a drawdown in April that set it back. But the more impressive phenomenon is gold massively outperforming equities.

Which makes it appear that, though equity investors have become more optimistic, aggregate capital keeps behaving defensively.

Risks

First of all, we're in that phase where monetary policy attempts to soft-land the economy after a period of aggressive tightening and it appears that it's been working so far. However, the jury's still out as there's more room for rates to fall and the labor market has been rapidly deteriorating lately. Fears of a recession don't seem elevated, but that's beside the point that the risk is elevated.

Even if the Federal Reserve achieves a soft landing, elevated valuations in equities could offset the sentiment benefits that this scenario would realize. Above, I implied that Big Tech exposure is advantageous if growth turns out to be below trend averages. That advantage has a long-term risk associated with it, which is the potential AI bubble. We certainly cannot ignore the likelihood that AI adoption can end up being slower than capital deployment, resulting in subpar growth, at best, and panic, at worst.

The other point to keep in mind is that sentiment is currently running hot. The index below shows that, as certain feared risks didn't materialize in 2025, such as a recession or an immediate and significant rise in inflation after higher tariffs were announced, investors started increasing their equity exposure again.

The issues implied here are that anything to disrupt the "Goldilocks" scenario of cooling inflation and a continuation in the latest growth rates can cause a sell-off, and that if nearly everyone is already in the market, who is left to buy?

Conclusion

For these reasons, I am maintaining my hold rating on SCHB. This isn't meant to express that one should be underweight stocks versus other assets like gold or Treasuries, but that an overweight position is not warranted yet. Based on the risks involved, a balanced approach to stay exposed to these assets, adjusted for historical volatility profiles, is reasonable.

Now, I am always open to challenging my views because being an ideologue in investing and trading can prove expensive. So if you have a different opinion, this platform is the right place to discuss both strategy and the various options with which we can position our portfolios. Go ahead and leave a comment below to let me know what you think.

Otherwise, please let me know if you found this article useful. It means a lot!

data.title: Viasat: A Satellite Leader With Room To Grow - Buy
data.description:
Introduction

Viasat (VSAT) is a satellite manufacturer that has operated for over 40 years, currently has 23 operational satellites in space, and earned $4.5B in revenue in fiscal year 2025. The firm’s satellites are used for purposes such as general aviation, government communications, information security, and cyber defense. They have been the recipient of a number of long-term contracts that can help boost revenue stability in the future. Overall, we rate VSAT a buy as there are significant tailwinds in the space sector and they have solid financials. In the below section, we will review some relevant trends and news that can impact VSAT.

Recent news and trends

VSAT is diversified in their portfolio and they are investing in R&D

The firm has multiple lines of businesses that meet the needs of many different customer segments. See the below image from their November 2025 investor presentation that highlights their customer segmentation map.

We believe this mix of product lines can help the firm weather any swings in governmental contracts or large client concentration risks, and this can boost investor confidence in VSAT. An additional positive facet that we noticed is the firm’s commitment to investing in research and development.

From this graph, you can see that the percentage of revenue across lines of business and their overall portfolio that is spent on advancing research is growing over the past year, and there is no reason that this growth will slow anytime soon.

With a strong backlog, VSAT is poised to continue investing in its development and growth in the future, and this combination of diverse portfolio mix with a vision for the future hits on important checkboxes for considering an investment in a company that works in the broader space infrastructure.

Space as an investment sector is gaining significant traction

Space as a sector has been given lots of attention in recent times. Just this week, the Department of Energy announced that there are plans to develop a fission reactor on the surface of the moon – by 2030! This incredibly aggressive goal is aimed at providing the necessary future power for sustained long-term moon operations, as well as a fueling stop for transit to Mars. Beyond just solidifying American interests and leadership in the space economy, we believe that these announcements from the government serve as powerful tailwinds for many space-related companies - VSAT included. There is an astronomical - no pun intended - opportunity for space related enterprises, and VSAT is well-positioned to play a part.

The firm has plans for a number of new satellites across different orbit patterns in space, and we believe that as the government continues its push into enhancing and developing the space economy, companies like VSAT will prosper.

VSAT is targeting government contracts

Recently, VSAT integrated a network of satellites from Inmarsat, a previous acquisition, and other partner satellites into a streamlined and unified network called a “Ka-Band” of satellites that are specifically focused on government and military applications. This newly formed network is compatible and interoperable with existing military satellite communication networks, which leads to a bullish view as positioning yourself for lucrative government contracts can lead to stable, long-term revenue growth. We are pleased to see that VSAT is innovating and posturing their platform to cater to the government, because that market remains one of the most attractive. Beyond just government applications, they are also expanding their business in the private sector - announcing just last week a deal with Evergreen Marine to upgrade their connectivity platforms to one powered by Inmarsat. These innovative deals allow VSAT to grow their business from multiple perspectives and should allow for further growth in the future. We are bullish on these partnerships and believe they lead to further credibility and gains down the road.

Valuation

To come to a valuation estimate for VSAT, we use a peer comparison of the EV/EBITDA ratios. EV/EBITDA is a good measure in this case as satellite companies are capital intensive and often carry large debt. EV/EBITDA filters out some of those impacts to get to the core valuation. We use the following companies as peers: EchoStar Corporation (SATS), Iridium Communications Inc. (IRDM), Globalstar, Inc. (GSAT), SES S.A. (SGBAF), Eutelsat Communications S.A. (EUTLF) and Telesat Corporation (TSAT).

VSAT

SATS

IRDM

GSAT

SGBAF

EUTLF

TSAT

EV/EBITDA (FWD)

We pulled the FWD and TTM metrics for EV/EVBITDA in order to understand what the valuation looks like both on a current and future looking basis.

Peer Average

EV/EBITDA (FWD)

EV/EBITDA (TTM)

We see that the peer average stats for both metrics are much higher than VSAT’s with implied upsides of ~250%. Acknowledging that SATS and GSAT seem to be large outliners, we remove their stats and look at the average of the remaining peers. This will help us to be more conservative in our estimates.

Peer Average

Implied Upside

Valuation

EV/EBITDA (FWD)

EV/EBITDA (TTM)

25.86%

$53.92

With the outliers removed we come to a valuation range of $53.92 - $64.58 which represents a 26% - 51% upside. If we had to choose one valuation, we go with $53.92. The FWD looking metrics might be true, but they are just projections and can have errors while the TTM metrics are based on real results.

To sum up, we estimate a valuation for VSAT of $53.92, representing a 25.86% upside.

Risks

High Debt

The company has a high debt of $7.08B and a debt to equity ratio of 154.78%. The company’s interest coverage ratio is 0.30, meaning that their interest expenses are roughly 3x their EBIT. This high debt could be a problem for the company’s ability to continue operations. However, if we use the EBITDA instead of the EBIT to calculate the interest coverage ratio, we get 3.59. This shows that on a cash basis, the company is able to manage their interest well.

Competition in the space

There are a number of competitors in the space economy - both established players and upstarts looking to make a name for themselves. We believe that the overall pie is certainly large enough to be split amongst many players, however, and with VSAT’s existing expertise, vision, and roadmap, along with their diversified business and portfolio, the firm remains in a strong position to execute.

Conclusion

To conclude, VSAT is a diverse satellite manufacturer with significant government relationships and private sector clients. Their roadmap and commitment to research and development is a sign that the firm wants to grow their business further, and with the massive tailwinds coming from the government with regards to innovations in space, we believe that VSAT is in a good position to capitalize.

Based on our analysis of the company’s growth trends as well as an estimated valuation of $53.92 we rate VSAT as a strong buy.

data.title: This Bull Run Looks Tired: If SPY Breaks Below $685, A Big Correction May Be Next
data.description:
It's quite interesting to see how fast narratives change in the current market. One day, investors are looking at the primary beneficiaries of the $602B CapEx that the 5 hyperscalers (4+Oracle) plan to spend this year, and the other, the headlines are mostly about why policy changes will tank US equities.

I admit that I am increasingly worried about the near-term continuation of the bull run. I see several signs that suggest a dramatic shift in sentiment.

No, I'm not predicting the all-time highs of the AI supercycle, but I am starting to believe that a correction, similar to what we have seen last year, is likely to happen.

For now, I'm still bullish, although I believe we are at a critical point; therefore, the rating downgrade. To be precise, if I see the SPY trading at $685 again, I will strongly downgrade my ratings. Below, I explain why.

Am I The Only One Seeing This Huge Downside Risk In The Charts?

Yes, yes, yes... technical analysis may be astrology for some investors, and, to some extent, I agree.

However, I use the most rudimentary TA concepts to gauge the sentiment in the markets, rather than trying to trade bizarre patterns (i.e., head and shoulders, cups, you name it).

During a bull run, I focus on higher highs and higher lows over a 4–24 week period. Provided earnings are rising at a double-digit rate (Q3 blended EPS in the S&P 500 was up 13% yoy, the 4th consecutive double-digit growth quarter), and the wall of worry remains manageable, I often maintain a positive view of the markets.

However, when markets fail to break to new highs despite intact fundamentals, I begin to sweat, as investor attention shifts towards the wall of worry.

I see this precise shift taking place right now. Let me explain.

The chart below represents the Nasdaq 100 (NDX) over the past 18 months.

I am not excited about that chart. Why?

First, we haven't seen new highs since the end of October. Coincidentally, that's when the broadening gained momentum. Aside from pockets of growth in memory, most stocks in the tech sector (particularly those within the IGPT software ETF) have been struggling to keep up with the S&P 500 index.

Second, take a look at what happened approximately 12 months ago (2). The NDX struggled to break new all-time highs for approximately 8 weeks. Once the upper trend line (defined by higher lows in the chart) was broken, a dramatic selloff followed.

The same pattern can be seen in the SPY:

This year, we don't have tariffs as the excuse for a similar dramatic selloff. That said, we have plenty of other reasons to give large institutional funds an excuse to take profits, deleverage risk, and head for the exit door.

The Wall Of Worry Is Building Up

In this article, I will mainly focus on policy risks. In my next articles, I will cover geopolitics, including the US interest in Greenland and the broader Middle East escalations.

First, the Fed's independence may be at risk once the new Fed Chair takes over in May. Prediction markets suggest the new Chair may be Kevin Warsh.

Back in October, Warsh made the following dovish remarks:

We can lower rates a lot and, in so doing, get 30-year fixed-rate mortgages so they're affordable ... Lower interest rates with the kind of technology revolution the president's policies have permitted, the massive investment that's happening in the economy domestically and from foreigners, this is the seed corn for our productivity revolution.

I highlighted a few words that caught my eye: "a lot" and "president's policies." Bear in mind that Trump said back in June that he wants 1% rates. It is my view that the new Fed Chair will be dovish and will drive interest rates down, even if the data is not necessarily supportive.

Big deal? Aren't low interest rates supposed to lift risk assets like equities?

Yes, but only to a certain extent during economic recessions. The biggest risk of lowering rates when the economy is healthy is inflation. Take a look at recent inflation rates in a few countries where central bank independence is widely seen as weak:

  • Venezuela: The IMF estimates that 2025 inflation could end around 548%.
  • Turkey: 31% at the end of last year.
  • Zimbabwe: 32.7% in October 2025.
  • Argentina: 31% last year.

So, what's the upside for the government if interest rates go down? Gaining votes from executives or investors in equities?

Not really. When Treasury yields fall, the government can issue new debt and refinance maturing debt at lower rates, reducing interest over time. Given that the deficit in December 2025 was $145B, a record for December, the government wants to see rates coming down, not just from a refinancing perspective, but also from a GDP growth perspective (lower rates reduce financing costs for households and firms, which increases the nominal GDP).

However, this could be bad for the economy.

None other than former Fed Chair Janet Yellen stated that using the Fed’s key overnight borrowing rate to manage the debt would be irresponsible, using the following terms:

It is the road to a banana republic

This topic (Fed independence) was addressed by Powell last Sunday in a response to the recent grand jury subpoenas, threatening a criminal indictment tied to Powell’s June Senate testimony about cost overruns on a $2.5B Fed HQ renovation project:

This new threat is not about my testimony last June or about the renovation of the Federal Reserve buildings. It is not about Congress's oversight role; the Fed through testimony and other public disclosures made every effort to keep Congress informed about the renovation project. Those are pretexts. The threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the President.

For now, inflation data has been mostly favorable. The December release showed a headline CPI of 2.7% yoy, in line with consensus. However, core CPI came in 10 bps below the consensus, at 2.6% yoy. Yes, we are above the Fed's 2% long-term target, but I believe that figure needs to be adjusted to a more realistic value. We haven't seen the CPI below 2% since February 2021!

What's key is that tariffs are not driving up inflation.

The risk here is that the Fed cuts rates, and we return to the hyperinflation levels of 2022, when the CPI was 9.1% yoy (June 2022).

Other risks include credit price controls after the proposed 10% cap on credit card rates. I already discussed my view on this risk in my BAC coverage.

Long story short, the key date to monitor is January 20. Similar to what happened with tariffs back in April last year, I believe we could see either one of the two scenarios:

  1. The credit rate cap is delayed.
  2. The carveouts limit the downside for the banks.

Of course, no one knows exactly what would happen. However, I certainly refuse to believe that such a big policy change will be implemented in a matter of a few days.

In an upcoming article, I will explain how I'm positioning my portfolio to capitalize on the selloff in the financial sector. I advance here that I'm hyperbullish, and I see a quick trade in the form of call options (by quick, I mean March 2026 expirations).

Finally, don't ignore the Supreme Court scrutiny of IEEPA tariffs. These include both the reciprocal global tariffs and the tariffs on China, Mexico, and Canada tied to a declared national emergency about fentanyl.

According to some sources, the government could be on the hook for approximately $150B in refunds if the Supreme Court deems these tariffs illegal. The risk here is not from a deficit perspective. What makes me sweat is the uncertainty. Markets don't like uncertainty, and even if the Supreme Court wins, there is a good chance Trump will use other vehicles to implement these tariffs again. This volatility could lead to a broader selloff.

Conclusion

Despite the recent buildout of the wall of worry, the 10-year yield is relatively stable and quite far from the 5% value that some sources suggested we were heading towards last year.

The charts are close to a key inflection point. I warn readers that seeing the SPY below $685 would make me reconsider my bullishness in the broader markets.

Keep a close eye on any policy developments in the US. A nice near-term catalyst could be the delay of the 10% cap on credit card rates, which has been pressuring the financial sector despite strong earnings results.

data.title: Trip.com: Anti-Monopoly Investigation Clouds The Sentiment
data.description:
Trip.com Group Limited (TCOM) stock dropped by -17% on the 14th of January. The online travel service provider received an investigation notice from market regulators in China regarding anti-monopoly laws, causing significant market-wide concern due to increased regulatory uncertainty. Aided by continued industry growth, TCOM’s financial performance has remained impressive.

I upgraded my rating to Buy in my previous August 2025 article on the stock, titled “Trip.com Q2 Preview: Capturing China's Inbound Travel Surge.” The stock has since lost 3% of its value, losing to the S&P 500’s 8% gain.

TCOM Received An Investigation Notice Regarding Anti-Monopoly Allegations

TCOM released a concerning announcement on the 14th of January. The company received a notice of investigation from the State Administration for Market Regulations “SAMR” in China. The market regulator has opened an investigation into TCOM regarding China’s anti-monopoly law over potential monopolistic behavior in the market. TCOM held around a 56% market share in the hotel and travel market in China as of 2024, giving weight to concerns over TCOM’s market power.

The development follows several parties’ previous complaints about TCOM’s alleged anti-competitive business practices. Zhengzhou’s market regulator previously summoned TCOM for a regulatory talk in September, citing the company’s service agreements, transaction rules, and technical measures to impose unreasonable restrictions on merchants' transactions and pricing. The Yunnan Tourism Homestay Association voiced similar concerns in December, for example, arguing that TCOM’s “pick-one” exclusivity for merchants is anti-competitive.

The formal SAMR investigation moves TCOM into regulatory uncertainty. The investigation could hypothetically lead to fines, compliance requirements, or operational changes for TCOM if SAMR ultimately finds TCOM to have abused its market power. The maximum fine that can be imposed under China's anti-monopoly law is 10% of a company’s global revenues in the latest year, which would currently add up to $730 million for TCOM. Considering the company’s $11.3 billion in cash and short-term investments, even the maximum fine wouldn’t impact TCOM very substantially. Potential operational restrictions could be more damaging for TCOM’s market position and financial performance.

Ultimately, the investigation has only started and is likely to take at least several months to come to a conclusion. No outcome is guaranteed as unlawful practices are currently only being alleged.

The Current Earnings Growth Outlook Stands Great, Aided By Industry Growth

TCOM’s financial performance has remained impressive. The company reported a 16% increase in revenues in Q3, stemming from a continued surge in inbound travel bookings and TCOM’s strong performance in outbound travel. Increased marketing investments and cost of revenue drove a small decrease in the margin level, but TCOM’s earnings power remained very strong regardless. Adjusted EBITDA came in at a 35% margin at $892 million for Q3, showing a very healthy 12% year-on-year increase. Current business momentum has been strong.

China’s travel industry growth continues to provide both short- and long-term tailwinds to TCOM. Domestic passenger flight volume increased by 6.6% in November as reported by CAAC, and international routes showed very fast 20.4% passenger volume growth. The pace has even slightly accelerated from monthly figures during Q3, positioning TCOM very well for Q4.

Beyond 2025, WTTC estimates that the Chinese travel and tourism sector will nearly double during the next ten years. The Chinese government’s more lenient stance on tourism, notably including visa waivers in 2025, positions the industry for good long-term growth.

TCOM’s Risk-to-Reward Is Still Good At Current Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for TCOM. I estimate faster growth for 2025 than previously at 20%, led by TCOM’s strong recent reports. Afterwards, I estimate a good growth continuation, implying a total 9.2% 2024-2034 CAGR. I very slightly lowered long-term revenue growth estimates from previous ones, not factoring in further market share gains after 2025.

I again assume TCOM to maintain high margins at a 27% long-term EBIT margin and that the company will convert earnings well into free cash flow due to the company’s capital-light business model.

The estimates put TCOM’s fair value at $89.3, 43% above the stock price at the time of writing. Current financial momentum again provides great upside for investors. The DCF model assumes that TCOM manages to maintain its strong market position but does not expand its market share further in China.

SAMR’s investigation could theoretically have negative implications on TCOM’s market performance, but such regulatory downside is more than offset by great base scenario upside. I believe that TCOM’s risk-to-reward is still good at the current valuation.

The fair value estimate is up slightly from $85.5 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 8.66%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a very low 1.77% interest rate, annualized from Q3 interest expenses and debt. I estimate TCOM’s debt to remain modest at a 10% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.15% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 5.14% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for China. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.00. With a liquidity premium of 0.1%, the cost of equity stands at 9.39% and the WACC at 8.66%.

Takeaway

SAMR’s investigation into TCOM adds regulatory uncertainty in the investment case, likely to increase volatility in at least the next few months after TCOM’s stock plummeted following the news. Given TCOM’s strong financial momentum, long-term industry tailwinds in China, and a relatively cheap valuation, I still believe that the investment’s risk-to-reward remains good. Despite regulatory uncertainty, I remain at a Buy rating for TCOM stock.

data.title: Tripadvisor: Strategic Pivot Underway, Compelling Upside Ahead
data.description:
Tripadvisor (TRIP) is a leading digital travel business best known for its vast library of user reviews and its hotel offerings. This is a sector we like, and even though we currently have only Expedia (EXPE) in our coverage, we decided to initiate this company with a buy recommendation. Beyond reviews and price comparisons, the company lets travelers book tours and activities on its direct website (Fig. 1), Tripadvisor.com and Viator, and make restaurant reservations in Europe through TheFork (formerly LaFourchette).

Source: Tripadvisor Q3 ppt - Fig. 1

Why are we positive?

We remain constructive for several reasons:

Tactically, sentiment has been hit after Trip.com Group disclosed it received an investigation notice from China’s State Administration for Market Regulation, dragging the broader industry down and putting it under near-term pressure.

The core Tripadvisor.com brand is facing traffic headwinds. Indeed, the Brand Tripadvisor top-line sales fell 8% in Q3 2025 and are expected to decline by low-teens in Q4 2025. That said, management’s push to better “convert” remaining top-of-funnel demand into transactions by more tightly linking Tripadvisor traffic to Viator is. And, in our view, this is a supportive evolution.

Source: Tripadvisor Q3 press release - Fig. 2

It is key to report the Starboard Value’s recent presentation (Fig. 3). For our readers, this is a prominent hedge fund that invests in undervalued public companies. Considering a weaker revenue trend (and following Starboard Value's indication), Tripadvisor has announced a restructuring program targeting roughly $85 million in gross savings. Here at the Lab, we believe Tripadvisor will be back to profitable growth. However, the company has ample margin to adjust its personnel cost basis.

Aside from potential layoffs, the company will proceed with operational integration between the core brand and Viator. This will likely improve margin momentum and, if executed, could lift near-term earnings while accelerating a longer-term pivot toward Experiences, our next chapter in the industry. Airbnb and Booking are already moving in this direction.

Source: Starboard Value - Fig. 3

Lastly, valuation matters. There is a sum-of-the-parts thesis, but it is essential to note that Tripadvisor will adjust segment reporting in Q4 (Fig. 4). This should improve transparency, even as it introduces near-term forecasting uncertainty. Despite a potential uplift in trading multiple (Fig. 5), valuation support is reinforced by sum-of-the-parts optionality. This is around the TheFork value, where profitability has been solid, and other initiatives to monetize the legacy brand’s data.

Fig. 4

Fig. 5

Here at the Lab, we have reviewed the main criticism from Wall Street equity research analysts. Skeptics could argue that the setup is less compelling, pointing to another headline: revenue down versus consensus expectations. There might be another cut to the guidance range, with Brand Tripadvisor falling by the low teens in Q4 2025. Additionally, we have taken into account the near-term margin pressure as restructuring actions and incremental investment spending flow through the P&L. Despite the difficulty of forecasting near-term headwinds, we believe the balance of risks and potential returns remains skewed to the upside.

Valuation

During Q3 results, the company's management team lowered its 2025 top-line sales outlook (3%-5% growth, down from the prior guidance of 5%-7%). Reverse-engineering its 2025 implied results means no change in Q4 2025 sales compared to Q4 2024 results. There was no change in the EBITDA margin projection expected to be ~16%-18%. Thus, sales are estimated at $1.9 billion and EBITDA at $300 million (using Tripadvisor's lower range).

A sum-of-the-parts framework inevitably rests on several execution-sensitive assumptions. But Starboard Value did this exercise:

Assuming TheFork gets sold at 5x CY26E revenue, Viator improves margins, and Brand Tripadvisor stabilizes growth and/or improves margins, the PF Company trades at just 2.5x EBITDA! (Fig. 6).

While this might be true, we believe that reduced guidance clearly implies a lower intrinsic value, but we think the current pricing offers an attractive entry point. There is limited leverage within the company. At this stage, the stock trades at 5x EV/EBITDA. We are positive on the sector, and even if we do not account for a margin uplift, we believe its valuation well justifies a 7x EV/EBITDA multiple. The company has historically traded at an average EV/adjusted EBITDA multiple of 15x before the pandemic. The multiple bottomed during this period, but with a ROIC above 30% and a double-digit FCF yield, there is room for a re-rating. With an EV/EBITDA of 7x, our equity value reached $2.1 billion. This represents a 31% upside to the current market cap with a target price of $18 per share.

Fig. 6

Risks

Tripadvisor's key risks include an inability to stabilize traffic and lower monetization and higher marketing costs (digital marketing). We also report weakening marketing efficiency and returns on ad spend. The company faces greater competition, which might be an overhang on growth and margins. Key to the report is a consumer slowdown and a macro pressure that could weigh on discretionary consumer spending. That said, we are constructive on growth in online experiences.

Conclusion

In a nutshell, we believe Tripadvisor is an asymmetric opportunity with the downside case already well priced in and many levers that support a valuation uplift (TheFork potential exit, Viator’s margins, monetizing Brand Tripadvisor’s data, and lower costs). We are positive on the sector, so we initiated the company with a clear buy.

data.title: South Korea ETFs: Tailwinds Carry Over From 2025
data.description:
By Roxanna Islam, CFA, CAIA

2025 was a significant year for international investing. Many portfolios keep international exposure in a core sleeve. However, single-country ETFs can work as targeted satellite allocations and can lead to exciting, niche opportunities. South Korea has been one of the countries drawing outsized attention, and the performance numbers explain why. The iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) returned over 100% in 2025. And many of the tailwinds from 2025 are expected to carry over into 2026. Below is what advisors and investors should know before investing in South Korea, including the main ETF tools to get exposure.

What’s Happening in Korea?

Performance is a big reason Korea has been on investors’ radar. In 2025, many Korea-focused ETFs were up 90-100% (in other words, they approximately doubled). But much of the surge was tied to two tech-heavy giants: Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) and SK hynix (HXSC.F), both of which are major players in the global chip supply chain. Samsung is best known as a consumer brand (e.g., smartphones and televisions). However, it also excels in memory chips (the storage/working memory that sits inside most electronics) and in foundry services (manufacturing chips for other companies). SK hynix is even more directly a memory specialist. It makes memory chips, including high-bandwidth memory (HBM: a type of ultra-fast memory increasingly paired with AI chips in data centers). They performed well because the AI boom boosted demand for advanced memory. When demand rises for a high-value component, pricing and profits can improve fast - especially for companies that sit at the center of that supply chain.

Korea’s global manufacturing footprint extends beyond chips into autos and industrials. For example, Korean ETFs also hold automakers like Hyundai Motor and Kia Corp. And more recently, Korea’s defense manufacturers like Hanwha Aerospace have also been a visible growth pocket inside Korea equity exposure. Hanwha positions itself across land/sea/air defense and aerospace.

Korea remains a cultural hub, but the ETF wrapper didn’t always translate into durable assets. The JAKOTA K-Pop and Korean Entertainment ETF (KPOP) liquidated around April 2025. That’s a useful reminder that thematic narratives can be compelling, but ETF outcomes still depend on liquidity, investor adoption, and other underlying fundamentals.

The Push for Developed Market Status

I expect the growth trajectory at both the company and sector level to continue, but an even bigger catalyst could come from index classification. South Korea is still classified as an emerging market by MSCI, which can feel counterintuitive given Korea’s economic status. The key issue, however, is market accessibility, and MSCI has pointed to Korea’s currency market frictions as a sticking point (in MSCI’s framework, developed market currencies trade globally 24 hours).

That’s why Korea’s latest push matters. The government has launched an initiative aimed at inclusion in the MSCI Developed Markets Index, including moving the FX market from extended hours to a 24-hour trading system.

South Korea: ETF Opportunities

Most broad Korea equity exposure is top-heavy. Performance is often driven by a handful of megacaps - especially semiconductors.

Investors have several ETF options:

  • The iShares MSCI South Korea ETF (EWY) offers broad, core exposure to South Korean equities. It tracks the MSCI Korea 25/50 Index, a top-heavy market-cap weighted index. Samsung and SK hynix together make up around 45% of the ETF’s weight. This ETF is the largest of its peers with almost $9.5 billion in assets. Its expense ratio is 0.59%.
  • The Franklin FTSE South Korea ETF (FLKR) offers a similar solution as EWY (large- and midcap Korea exposure) with a different underlying index. FLKR follows the FTSE South Korea RIC Capped Index. It has a significantly lower expense ratio than EWY (9 bps vs 59 bps).
  • The Matthews Korea Active ETF (MKOR) provides active Korea exposure. While Samsung and SK hynix are still weighted at the top of the holdings list, the ETF has more flexibility to deviate from market cap relative to an indexed ETF like EWY and FLKR.
  • The PLUS Korea Defense Industry Index ETF (KDEF) stands out from the group as a more focused thematic/industry play on the Korean defense sector. Exposure to Asian defense can complement U.S. and European exposure, especially given Asia’s tech innovation in cybersecurity and other defense technology. Recently, I wrote about the surging interest in international defense ETFs, which included the launch of KDEF and the WisdomTree Asia Defense Fund ETF (WDAF). WDAF is broader than KDEF, but still has South Korea as around 35% of its weight.
  • Direxion Daily South Korea Bull 3X Shares (KORU) seeks 300% daily return of the MSCI Korea 25/50 Index (which is the same index followed by EWY). Leveraged ETFs are typically used for short-term positioning and not a buy-and-hold allocation.

Bottom Line

South Korea could continue to provide an exciting international satellite opportunity in 2026. Korea’s rally has been tightly linked to a small set of megacap exporters - especially semiconductors. Samsung’s positioning in memory/foundry and SK hynix’s push into next-gen memory help explain why Korea exposure can move fast when the AI cycle accelerates.

Disclosure: © VettaFi LLC 2026. All rights reserved. This material has been prepared and/or issued by VettaFi LLC ("VettaFi") and/or one of its consultants or affiliates. It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit www.vettafi.com

data.title: Rocket Lab Enters A Different League
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) enters 2026 as one of the very few vertically integrated vertical space primes that truly matters in the space of national security. RKLB has appreciated over 100% from my last coverage, not driven by speculation but on market acceptance.

Space Systems has become the growth engine, the backlog has become a multi-year business, and Electron has set a standard of reliability. Neutron is no longer about vision; it’s about scale, unlocking larger missions and larger deals. Execution will ultimately determine whether Rocket Lab has truly crossed the line from new space to critical infrastructure, and if it does, the business will merit a durable re-rating rather than a speculative premium.

The Real Worth of Electron's Stock Will No Longer Be Growth

Launches no longer excite Electron, and that’s why it’s so valuable. Rocket Lab has had 21 successful launches in 2025 with a perfect success rate of their missions, which makes them one of only three companies that have been able to reach orbit multiple times in a year, along with SpaceX and ULA. But reliability isn’t something new; it’s expected.

This is what Rocket Lab's moat is. The function of Electron is no longer a growth engine but a trust engine. Electron is a guarantor of integrity with government agencies, facilitates rapid response missions like HASTE, and sustains improvement in margins by overhead absorption with acceleration of the rate. The company reiterated that Electron is designed for scale with a rate of up to 52 per year and that supply chain risk is effectively zero since it does everything.

Backlog is the proof point that the reliability offered by Electron is already being leveraged for revenue. The gradual rise to above $500 million, an increase of 56% year-over-year, indicates that customers are committing sooner and more confidently in order to grow Electron from a launch service to a demand anchor for the overall Rocket Lab platform.

Margins Are Rising But Earnings Power Isn’t (Yet)

One area where the company definitely showed improvement was in gross margins. The GAAP gross margin came in at 37%, the non-GAAP at 41.9%, while the Q4 guidance is even higher than that. It is good to note that these improvements are not just accounting-related but also come from improvements in the mix of business and the number of product launches.

However, one should not confuse margin progress with a company’s maturity. It is clear from management’s guidance that Q3 included a benefit of a cancellation of a contract at a margin of near 100%, together with a change in revenue recognition related to HASTE missions. These are not concerns but indicators of a company with very good contract management discipline. However, these are not factors that sustain earnings power.

Far more importantly, Rocket Lab is still substantially cash flow negative. It has high operating expenses because it is working on its Neutron program, and while its GAAP EPS has sequentially improved, a substantial part of that is because of its acquisition accounting treatment, which gave it a tax benefit.

That is accounting leverage, not operating leverage. Hence, this is encouraging, but the company is still on the investment side and not on the harvesting side yet. The market, on the other hand, is already pricing the harvest.

The Neutron is the Entire Thesis Compressed into a Schedule

Everything from the 2026 investment scenario ends up going through Neutron. The comments from management concerning Neutron were so measured as to be almost insubordinate. Rocket Lab will not rush Neutron to the launch site to meet other people’s timelines. They will qualify and accept tests to their internal timelines, even to the extent of delaying. In the space industry, reputation expands exponentially compared to revenue, while issues last longer than delays.

Despite this, it also holds some risks for the successful implementation of this launch. The launch will take place in Q1 2026. This timeline isn’t fixed, however, because it will depend on the things they can learn from fueling tests and so on. The Neutron’s presence at the pad is also set to happen during Q1 of 2026.

From the financial point of view, Neutron has overrun its development cost budget. The cumulative spend is expected to be $360 million by the end of 2025, which is much higher than the original guidance provided. This does not pose a problem in itself but indicates the importance of the Neutron program to the capital-intensive business of Rocket Lab in the short term.

Importantly, even as Rocket Lab remains a capital-intensive business, capex as a percentage of revenue is expected to decline materially as Neutron transitions from development into production and revenue contribution.

A Valuation Multiple Built on Perfect Execution

At today’s prices, Rocket Lab’s forward P/S multiple is about 82x on 2025 revenue and in the mid-50s on 2026 forecasts. Even under very positive assumptions, multiple expansion does most of the heavy lifting in terms of getting to a reasonable valuation in the long run. By 2027-2028, consensus forecasts tighten the sales multiple in the 30s as revenue grows past $1.2-1.5 billion.

This implies that there is a presumption of continued growth in excess of 30%, a smooth conversion of the backlog, and that there are no material slippages in either Neutron or defense programs. This is doable, but very tight. It does not have any kind of margin for error in valuation.

This is why Rocket Lab does not screen well on traditional valuation metrics despite its improving fundamentals. The market is no longer discounting the future. It is pre-paying for it.

The Dominant Risk Is Timing, Not Demand

What poses the greatest risk for Rocket Lab in 2026 is not competition, funding, or market size. It’s the risk of timing, and it’s all about Neutron. The demand is real. The backlog is real. The defense relevance is real. The part in question is how the initial rate of flight of the Neutron satisfies market expectations to avoid a story reset.

The secondary risk is related to lumpiness in Space Systems. The Defense sector transitions in a step fashion. Perhaps there will be a slight delay in contracts or milestone payments, which may cause lumpiness in terms of quarter-over-quarter expansion, even if overall expansion is strong. Such an issue will be ignored at a lower price point. It will be in focus at these price points.

These do not threaten the long-term survivability of Rocket Lab. These threats are to the future that investors are currently paying for.

Takeaway

RKLB is no longer a stock to be valued on potential but on delivery. The chance for 2026 is dependent on Neutron proving out scale while Space Systems compounds beneath the radar. If so, RKLB will have earned its multiple; otherwise, market turmoil follows.

data.title: KRE: Regional Banks Even Cheaper, But Also Outperforming
data.description:
Financials are off to a rough start to the year. Among the 11 S&P 500 sectors, it has the worst return, albeit just through 10 trading days. But one niche, regional banks, has outperformed.

I had a buy rating on the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Regional Banking ETF (KRE) last summer, and shares have returned a solid 5.4% since then, lagging the S&P 500 just two percentage points. Over the last six months, however, not only has KRE beaten the SPX, but it has also outpaced the broader Financials Select Sector ETF (XLF).

I reiterate a buy rating. The yield curve remains steeper compared to recent years; small businesses could see their fundamental conditions improve (thanks to the One Big Beautiful Bill Act), and ongoing hot US GDP growth is an upside catalyst. What's more, interest rates overall are very stable, helping to improve general risk-taking confidence.

The midterm-year loser so far is clearly Financials (XLF), the worst S&P 500 sector in 2026

2s10s has dipped below 60 bps this morning, the flattest since Dec 10

KRE Beating XLF and SPY Over the Last 6 Months

According to the issuer, KRE seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the total return performance of the S&P Regional Banks Select Industry Index, which is a modified equal-weighted index that provides the potential for unconcentrated industry exposure across large, mid, and small-cap stocks.

KRE is a medium-sized ETF with $4.3 billion in assets under management as of January 15, 2026. That’s up from $3.8 billion at the time of my August 2025 assessment. Its annual expense ratio is low to moderate at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is 2.37%--more than a full percentage point above that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum is not rated all that well by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, but with the fund flirting with multi-year highs and posting some alpha to the broader market since the second half of last year, I’d argue that the momentum situation has improved markedly. What’s more, liquidity is great with KRE—average daily volume is north of 16 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at just two basis points, per State Street Funds.

But KRE is also highly risky. Though the portfolio is not heavily concentrated, it is tied to a single industry that is among the most economically exposed in the market. Also, without international exposure, a further fall in the dollar could result in underperformance relative to, say, the All-Country World Index.

For an update on the portfolio, the 1-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar is heavily allocated to the small-cap value corner of the style box. That means cyclical optimism is bullish for the fund, but if we see negative revisions to GDP and corporate earnings, that could spell bad news for regional banks.

As it stands, the price-to-earnings ratio is very low at just 10.6x--down 0.6 figures from my Q3 2025 analysis. And with a long-term earnings growth rate of 13.1%, the resulting PEG ratio is very attractive, under 1.

KRE: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

For a closer look at the portfolio breakdown, the 100% Financials-sector fund has a modified equal weight structure, so no single name will carry an outsized position. That doesn’t mean volatility won’t come about, especially right now—in the heart of regional banking earnings season.

I’d call out that KRE paid out a record amount of total dividends last year, which is appealing if you’re an income investor.

KRE: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, we are in a weak period. The late January through early April stretch has historically brought about volatility and corrections. So, calendar trends are certainly a bearish factor to consider in the very short term.

KRE: Weak Q1 Seasonal Trends

The Technical Take

With an improved valuation and some modest outperformance to the broader US stock market since July 2025, KRE’s technical situation is good—but not quite there yet to bullish territory. Notice in the chart below that shares still face key resistance at the $70 market—a spot I pointed out last time. If we see an upside breakout, a bullish technical target just above $90 would be triggered, based on the depth of the November 2024-April 2025 plunge and presumed subsequent breakout.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average—it's on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. What’s more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is in bullish territory—near 70 but not overbought. A breakout could lead to a test of the all-time high of $79 notched in late 2021.

KRE: On Breakout Watch, $79 Could Be In Play This Year

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on KRE. I see the regional bank ETF as compelling on valuation, while the technicals point to a possible breakout as the first quarter presses on and Q4 earnings reports roll in.

data.title: Understanding CLOB: Income From The Middle Of The CLO Structure
data.description:
The VanEck AA-BB CLO ETF (CLOB) is an actively managed ETF launched in September 2024. It buys AA to BB rated CLO debt tranches. Its goal is current income with capital preservation. CLOB charges 0.45% and pays monthly distributions, currently 6.59%. CLOB's AUM is $152.36 million, with 52 holdings as of January 10, 2026.

This mezzanine-level CLO debt can pay higher yields compared to similarly rated corporate bonds. That is because they come with certain additional risks not present in corporate bonds. However, CLOB comes with its own set of protections as well, which include overcollateralization (OC) tests and interest-coverage (IC) tests. These are designed to limit credit losses before they hit higher tranches.

What is a CLO?

“CLO” stands for “Collateralized Loan Obligation.” A CLO owns senior secured loans as collateral and issues multiple layers (“tranches”) of securities against that collateral. It then pays cash flows on a priority basis (“the waterfall”). CLOB does not buy the underlying loans directly; instead, it buys CLO tranches—the debt slices issued by CLO vehicles—across the AA and BB stack.

This has two practical consequences. First, this positions you one step removed from the borrower. The CLO structure—its protections, manager actions, and deal tests—sits in between. So your credit experience is coming from this CLO structure and not just the loan default rate.

Second, ratings distributions become important. VanEck’s portfolio has the following: AA 42.02%, A 22.85%, BBB 20.98%, and BB 13.49%, with 0.69% not rated (and a total investment grade share of 85.86%). This tells you where you sit in the credit stack and how strongly you are protected before losses reach you. The more weight given to higher-rated tranches, the less likely that loan losses will hurt cash flow or principal—at the fund level and at your investor level. CLOB lives mainly in the “upper mezzanine” and “lower IG” parts of the CLO debt stack, although it still goes into BB when the spread becomes interesting.

Sources of Distributions

CLOB’s distribution history helps us understand how the amounts and sources of distribution can vary month to month. It also tells us how CLO tranche cash flows and trading dynamics actually work.

Here are a few select examples of 2025 monthly distributions: $0.3049 on July 1, $0.3046 on August 1, $0.2754 on September 2, $0.2640 on October 1, $0.2836 on November 3, $0.2629 on November 28, and $0.3271 on December 29. November shows two distributions due to calendar timing, a common feature of monthly ETFs, rather than a change in underlying income. Annual payout (TTM) was $3.35 per share, or roughly 6.6% TTM yield.

As you can see, there’s a bit of variability month-to-month in the distributions. This variability comes from how these distributions are sourced. These are mainly two: net interest income from CLO tranches and realized gains or losses from trading those tranches.

VanEck’s distribution history helps understand this better since it also describes the composition breakdown of the distribution. For example, in the December 29, 2025 distribution, VanEck reports that $0.2630 of the payment was classified as dividend income, while $0.0641 was classified as short-term capital gains, with no return of capital.

This is an important distinction. CLOB’s core way to generate returns is the floating-rate coupon income coming from the CLO debt. But the manager (PineBridge) can also produce gains (or losses) by buying and selling the portfolio as spreads tighten (or widen). These CLO tranches are traded instruments, and their prices move with credit spreads, rate expectations, and relative value within the capital stack. When there are gains made, investors get a share as part of the monthly distribution. This is why distributions vary month to month.

Portfolio Holdings

From the fund fact sheet, here’s a quick list of some of the top holdings, along with coupons, maturities, and weights (along with a T-bill position):

  • Octagon 64 Ltd 5.570% maturity 07/21/2037 at 6.66% of net assets

  • Rockford Tower CLO 2022-1 Ltd 5.584% maturity 07/20/2035 at 4.67%

  • Dryden 95 CLO Ltd 6.939% maturity 08/20/2034 at 4.61%

  • United States Treasury Bill maturity 01/27/2026 at 4.59%

  • Several other CLO positions around 3.33%–3.35% each

The top ten holdings account for 41.55% of the portfolio.

From this list, we see that many of the top holdings have long legal maturities. However, these are floating rate coupons, so cash flow keeps changing regularly as rates change. That long maturity date simply tells you how long the deal lasts. We also see a bit of cash/T-bill, which is useful to avoid selling less liquid CLO tranches at lower prices when shares are created or redeemed.

Why these tranches pay more than corporate bonds

CLOB’s core goal is that it will yield more than similarly rated corporate bonds while still retaining built-in protection against credit loss. That extra yield is basically what investors earn for the additional risks—which don’t show up clearly if you only compare AA-rated CLOs to AA-rated corporate bonds. There are some practical reasons CLO debt can pay more than similarly rated corporates:

Complexity premium: Corporate bonds are easier to understand—one issuer, one balance sheet. CLO tranches are complex—a pooled loan basket, active management, reinvestment rules, structural tests, the works. That means fewer buyers will want to invest, so spreads widen.

Liquidity premium: In comparison to corporate bonds, CLO tranches may trade in instruments with much lower liquidity, even though ratings may be similar. Investors demand extra spread as compensation for that liquidity risk.

Reputational risk: CLOs are quite different from pre-crisis subprime CDOs, but they are both “structured finance.” So they may seem risky by association and keep investors away. This could widen spreads even when “default probability” may not warrant it. That means it could widen the yield even when ratings are the same.

Structural risk is different from issuer risk: CLOs are exposed to other sorts of risks like collateral quality migration, manager behavior, and deal test dynamics. It is not just the simple probability of one issuer defaulting. So even if both have the same rating, risk difference = premium difference.

This is important to understand: the extra yield you get comes at a price—extra risk and difficult-to-model risk. Many investors want to avoid that by default.

Those “built-in protections” and what they really mean

VanEck assures us that CLOB comes with “built-in protection against credit loss.” Don’t treat that phrase as a guarantee; it simply means that the fund’s CLO tranches are positioned above layers of subordinate capital. They are also governed by structural safeguards such as overcollateralization and interest-coverage tests that are designed to absorb losses before they reach senior investors.

What you can safely say (based on what’s shown):

  • CLOB mostly owns investment-grade tranches (~85.86% at last read);
  • Because these are tranches, losses will first hit the most subordinate layers before they hit higher layers—this is the core “cushion” logic;
  • The fund very clearly buys from AA+ down to BB- (or unrated but comparable quality).

What you cannot say is that CLOB is “safe like Treasuries.” The fund holds (97.15%) securitized credit, meaning its returns are exposed to credit spreads, liquidity issues, and structured-finance dynamics. Even when underlying loan performance remains stable, CLO tranche prices can fluctuate as risk appetite and market conditions change.

Peer Positioning: CLOB Versus AAA CLO ETFs

Seeking Alpha’s peer comparison places CLOB alongside CLO ETFs that invest almost exclusively in AAA-rated CLO tranches. The three names I want to highlight are Invesco AAA CLO Floating Rate Note ETF (ICLO), iShares AAA CLO Active ETF (CLOA), and PGIM AAA CLO ETF (PAAA).

These AAA CLO ETFs sit at the top of the capital structure. They are the last to absorb credit losses. They emphasize capital preservation, with yield being the trade-off. Recent yield data for these three are 5.48%, 5.34%, and 5.11%, respectively. These sit at the most protected level of the stack, so the spread is the narrowest.

CLO deliberately stays below AAA. As I discussed earlier, this positioning adds new risks while still retaining meaningful protection. In exchange, CLOB offers higher income, at around 6.5%, roughly 100-150 basis points higher than AAA-only CLO ETFs.

That 1-1.5% yield gap may look modest, but it is not. Relative to a 5.3% baseline, a 100 bps extra means nearly 20% extra income, which compounds meaningfully over time. More importantly, there’s not really a dramatic increase in expected default loss between AAA and AA-BB. It is simply meaningful extra compensation for moving down the capital stack without a meaningful increase in risks in most situations. Briefly then, AAA CLO ETFs primarily focus on capital preservation and liquidity, while CLOB is an income-oriented structured-credit strategy.

It is also useful to contrast CLOB with CLO equity-focused funds like Eagle Point Credit Company (ECC), Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC), and OFS Credit Company (OCCI). These invest in the equity tranche that lies at the bottom of the capital stack in terms of loss risks. CLO equity absorbs first losses and gets only whatever cash flow remains but can receive very high yields. OXLC and ECC, for example, have current yields of 32.52% and 29.96%, respectively. CLOB, on the other hand, has a much lower yield but sits higher in the structure and gets paid before equity. Each has its value at different market conditions, with CLOB serving as a balance of sorts.

Key Risks to Understand

CLOB comes with mostly IG-grade ratings and built-in protections, but it is still a structured credit instrument and carries risks that are different from those of traditional bonds.

Spread and liquidity risk: CLOB is priced mainly on credit spreads, not interest rates. When the market is risk averse, spreads can widen. Now, since these tranches have relatively lower liquidity, prices can move suddenly. This can happen even when there are no realized credit losses.

Reinvestment and vintage risk: How a CLO performs is partly dependent on how loan cash flows are reinvested. Future cash flows get affected by loan spreads, underwriting standards, and market conditions. Therefore, CLOs with the same ratings can perform differently if issued in different market conditions.

Manager and structural risk: CLOB is actively managed. Managers select the loans; they make trading decisions, and so on. So, CLOB’s performance may be influenced by its managers. On top of that, structural features such as OC/IC tests vary by deal. They can go down if collateral quality falls.

Conclusion

CLOB is best seen as an income-focused instrument concentrated in the upper mezzanine of the CLO capital stack. They are not substitutes for traditional bonds or cash. CLOB primarily invests in AA to BBB tranches, with some BB when spreads are good. They get a 1-1.5% yield premium from structure, subordination, and market frictions over AAA-rated ETFs, not because of dramatically higher expected credit losses.

On the other hand, that yield advantage is not trivial, as I have outlined here. They come with meaningful protection. Returns come mostly from carry, with periodic pass-through realized gains also contributing.

In terms of safety, therefore, CLOB sits near but below T-bills but much above equity CLOs. For investors who understand CLO waterfalls and are comfortable with some volatility in exchange for higher income, CLOB occupies a clear and defensible place in an income-oriented portfolio.

data.title: All Is Not Well On The Japanese Bond Market Front
data.description:
It should come as no surprise that the Japanese government bond market appears to be melting down and that the Japanese yen is very much on the back foot. After all, Sanae Takaichi, the new Japanese prime minister, is proposing a reckless budget policy and early elections at a time when her country is drowning in debt. What is surprising, however, is how calm world financial markets seem to be about the troubles surfacing in the world’s second-largest government bond market with a total outstanding debt of over $7 trillion. A full-blown Japanese government bond and currency market crisis could have major spillover effects on the rest of the world’s capital market and could renew US-Japanese trade tensions.

Anyone doubting that a Japanese government bond and currency market crisis is brewing has not been paying attention. Since March 2024, when the Bank of Japan’s (BOJ) yield curve control policy ended, the 10-year Japanese government bond yield has approximately tripled from 0.75 percent to 2.18 percent. That rate is the highest in the past 20 years. Meanwhile, over the past nine months, the Japanese yen has weakened by more than 10 percent from 142 yen to the dollar in April 2025 to around 159 yen to the dollar at present. That rate is the weakest for the Japanese yen in more than three decades and is bound to raise red flags in Washington about an unduly weak Japanese yen.

The underlying reason for the recent Japanese government bond market weakness is the abandonment of the BOJ’s yield control policy due to rising domestic inflation and the very poor state of the country’s public finances. At around 230 percent of GDP, Japan’s public debt is by far the highest among the industrialized countries. There appears to be little prospect that this debt mountain will be reduced anytime soon. The government continues to run a primary budget deficit, and the country’s long-term economic growth prospects are highly constrained by its poor demographics. With a shrinking workforce, Japan’s long-run economic growth potential is generally estimated to be below one percent.

Seeming to be casting caution to the wind, Sanae Takaichi is proposing a US$135 billion budget stimulus package that would add to the country’s budget deficit. She also appears to be on the verge of calling a snap election to strengthen her hold on power. Those elections would delay the budget’s approval for this fiscal year and would add pressure to the government bond and currency markets.

All of this puts the BOJ in an unenviable position. With inflation running at three percent, or above the BOJ’s two percent inflation target, and with the government pursuing an expansionary budget policy, the BOJ can ill-afford to reduce interest rates. At the same time, any effort to stabilize the currency and to fight inflation with higher interest rates would add to the budget deficit by increasing the government’s interest payment expenditures. That would raise further questions about Japanese public debt sustainability.

Against this background, it is difficult to understand how sanguine world financial markets are about Japan’s bond market troubles. Should those troubles deepen, one would think that they would induce global investors to scale back on risk and to cast their eye on other countries, not least the United States that also has unsustainable public finances. One would also think that markets would become concerned about the unwinding of the Japanese yen currency trade, whereby investors borrowed in yen to invest in higher-yielding international bonds. A repatriation of those funds would deprive the world of a major pillar of support that their financial markets have enjoyed from Japanese investment abroad. That would especially be the case if the Japanese banks were forced to sell their US government bond holdings to help cope with the large unrealized losses that they have on their Japanese government bond holdings.

Markets also seem to be ignoring the risk that a weakening Japanese yen could invite a US protectionist backlash against Japan. Heightening the chances of such a backlash is the fact that Japan is running a large bilateral trade surplus with the United States.

In short, troubles in the Japanese bond and currency market could have spillover effects on the rest of the world economy. World financial markets seem to be ignoring those risks at their peril.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
